g.data-narration: Deadlock prevention ensures at least one of the four necessary conditions cannot hold. Each condition can be attacked with specific strategies to structurally prevent deadlock.
g.text: Negating the four necessary conditions to prevent deadlock

g.data-narration: Attacking Mutual Exclusion: sharable resources like read-only files do not need mutual exclusion. But some resources like printers are intrinsically non-sharable. Cannot always apply.
Attacking Hold and Wait: require process to request all resources before execution starts. Or release all held resources before requesting new ones. Low utilization and starvation possible.
g.text: Request ALL resources at start, OR

g.data-narration: Attacking No Preemption: if a process holding resources requests one that cannot be allocated, release all its current resources. Process restarts when all resources available.
g.text: Cannot get new resource? Release all held

g.data-narration: Attacking Circular Wait: impose a total ordering on all resource types. Each process must request resources in increasing order of enumeration. Prevents cycles in resource graph.
Circular wait prevention is most commonly used. Assign a number to each resource. Process must request resources in order of their assigned numbers. Lock ordering in practice.

Side effects of prevention: reduced device utilization, reduced throughput, and possible starvation. Negating conditions constrains how processes use resources. Trade-off between safety and efficiency.
Deadlock prevention negates one or more necessary conditions. Circular wait ordering is most practical. Each method has trade-offs in utilization and flexibility.
g.text: Negate: Mutex | Hold-Wait | No-Preempt | Circular Wait (most practical)
